annotations.text: Did this patient have a community-acquired pneumonia? The increased frequency of community-acquired pneumonias is well recognized in patients infected with HIV. The most common pathogens are  *Streptococcus pneumoniae* and  *Haemophilus influenzae* , but other pathogens, such as  *Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Staphylococcus aureus, Branhamella catarrhalis, Rhodococcus equi* , and group B streptococcus, have also been identified with increased frequen

Since  *Pneumocystis carinii* is the most common single pathogen causing pneumonia in patients with AIDS, occurring in 80 percent of such patients during the course of their illness, I have to include it in the differential diagnosis of this patient's disease
Several features of the case, however, are inconsistent with this diagnosis. Crackles and wheezing are rarely heard on examination of the chest in patients with pneumocystis pneumonia.

The nonproductive cough, dyspnea, and fever, the widened arterialalveolar gradient, and the radiographic pattern of diffuse interstitial pulmonary infiltrates are all consistent with pneumonia caused by  *P. carinii.
Both transbronchial biopsy and bronchoalveolar lavage have a diagnostic sensitivity of at least 85 percent when used separately and 100 percent when used together.
Another opportunistic pathogen frequently encountered in association with  *P. carinii* in lung tissue of patients with AIDS is cytomegalovirus

Although cytomegalovirus is frequently recovered from respiratory tract secretions or on histologic examination of lung specimens of patients with AIDS, there have been only a few reports of cytomegalovirus pneumonitis without evidence of a concomitant pathogen.
Fever and dyspnea are frequent in patients with pulmonary toxoplasmosis, but cough and sputum production are usually absent.
the absence of involvement of other organs, such as the central nervous system, and the negative serologic test make the diagnosis of pulmonary toxoplasmosis improbable.
In trying to narrow the differential diagnosis I shall now focus on the radiographic finding of pulmonary reticulonodular infiltrates with questionable hilar lymphadenopathy.

The diseases with which an association of lung infiltrates and intrathoracic lymphadenopathy can be seen with any frequency in the AIDS population include mainly mycobacterial infections, fungal infections, Kaposi's sarcoma, and lymphoproliferative disorders.
Although intrathoracic lymphadenopathy has been reported in patients infected with  *P. carinii* this association is rare and should suggest a concomitant fungal infection or Kaposi's sarcoma
Kaposi's sarcoma is the most frequently encountered tumor in persons with AIDS, affecting about 35 to 48 percent of them.

The disease is often shown to be diffuse on radiographs of the chest, and two patterns that seem to correlate with the histologic findings have been described — poorly marginated, nodular-appearing densities and linear interstitial densities.
Hilar lymphadenopathy has been reported in 8 to 19 percent of patients with pulmonary Kaposi's sarcoma.
Since a fairly well organized miliary pattern would be very unusual in both Kaposi's sarcoma and non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, I shall dismiss both diagnoses.
I come to the possibility of mycobacterial disease in this patient.
It is believed that because of the profound dysfunction in T-cell activity in patients with HIV infection, mycobacterial disease in patients with AIDS presents with particular features.
The findings on radiographs of the chest are usually atypical, with diffuse infiltrates being more frequently encountered than upper-lobe cavitary disease, at least in late HIV infection.
Disease due to  *Mycobacterium avium–intracellulare* (also called  *M. avium* complex) is known to be prevalent among patients with AID
As opposed to tuberculosis, infection with  *M. avium–intracellulare* occurs late in HIV disease, when other AIDS-related complications are often present
The radiographs of the chest frequently show nodular, diffuse or patchy infiltrates, with or without hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
A miliary pattern, however, is very unusual.
I am finally led to consider infection with  *Histoplasma capsulatum* in this patient
Histoplasmosis has been reported in patients with AIDS from areas where this infection is endemic and from areas where it is not.

Patients from areas where it is not endemic almost uniformly report travel to areas of endemic infection, which include the Caribbean islands, Central and South America, and the Ohio and Mississippi River valleys in the United States.

Although histoplasmosis is often disseminated at the time of presentation and symptoms can be severe, mimicking a gram-negative septicemia, subacute illness with a high fever, substantial weight loss, and minor respiratory tract symptoms is not uncommon.
Fever and weight loss are the most frequent presenting manifestations, occurring in about 80 percent and 50 percent of the patients, respectively.
In a large recently reported series of cases, fever and weight loss were the presenting manifestations in 96 percent of the patients.
Did this patient have progressive disseminated histoplasmosis?

Although I cannot rule out miliary tuberculosis on the basis of the findings, I favor progressive disseminated histoplasmosis as the principal diagnosis in this case because of the history of recent previous residence in an area where the infection is endemic and the relatively slow evolution of the disease, with minimal respiratory tract symptoms.
